rule.desc: Never export TypeVar definitions in module __all__; TypeVars are implementation details, not public API.
rule.rationale: TypeVars are internal type machinery used for generic definitions. Exporting them pollutes the module's public API and suggests they should be used by callers, which is incorrect. With Python 3.12+ syntax, TypeVars become even more internal as they're declared inline.

example.code:
# Good - TypeVars not exported (pre-3.12 code) from typing import TypeVar, Generic
T = TypeVar("T") K = TypeVar("K") V = TypeVar("V")
__all__ = ["DataTool", "Parser", "Producer"] # Only public classes
class DataTool(Generic[T]): """Generic I/O tool.""" ...
class Parser(Generic[T]): """Generic parser.""" ...

# Good - Python 3.12+ (no TypeVars to export) __all__ = ["DataTool", "Parser", "Producer"]
class DataTool[T]: """Generic I/O tool.""" ...
class Parser[T]: """Generic parser.""" ...

# Bad - exporting TypeVars from typing import TypeVar, Generic
T = TypeVar("T")
__all__ = ["DataTool", "T"] # BAD: T should not be exported
class DataTool(Generic[T]): """Generic I/O tool.""" ...

rule.tool_support: ruff can check __all__ consistency with PLE0604.

notes.note: When migrating to Python 3.12+ syntax, remove all exported TypeVars as they're no longer defined separately.
Even if a TypeVar appears in public function signatures, don't export it. Users should reference the generic class/function, not the TypeVar directly.
